story_prompt: create a lecture on javascript function

scenes.text: Scene 1 introduces JavaScript functions. A function is a block of code designed to perform a particular task and is executed when it is called or invoked. Functions help in organizing and reuse code, making programs more efficient and manageable.
scenes.image_prompt: flowchart TD; A[Start] --> B[Define Function]; B --> C[Invoke Function]; C --> D[Function Execution]; D --> E[End];

scenes.text: Scene 2 explains function declaration and syntax. In JavaScript, a function is defined with the function keyword, followed by a name, parentheses, and a block of code between curly braces. Parameters can be added inside the parentheses to allow passing of arguments.
scenes.image_prompt: sequenceDiagram; actor User; participant JavaScript; User->>JavaScript: Declare function; JavaScript-->>User: function greet(){...};

scenes.text: Scene 3 covers function expressions. Unlike declarations, function expressions can be defined as part of a larger expression. They are not hoisted, meaning they cannot be called before being defined.

Scene 4 explores the concept of function return values. Functions can return a value back to the caller using the return statement. This allows the result of the function to be stored in a variable or used in further expressions.
scenes.image_prompt: flowchart TD; A[Function Call] --> B[Function Execution]; B --> C{Return Value?}; C -- Yes --> D[Pass value to caller]; C -- No --> E[Return undefined];

scenes.text: Scene 5 demonstrates anonymous functions and arrow functions. Anonymous functions don't have a name and are useful when a function is to be used only once. Arrow functions provide a concise syntax and do not have their own 'this' context.
scenes.image_prompt:
journey
    title Anonymous And Arrow Functions
    section Anonymous Functions
      User: --> Use once
      User: --> Combine with other code
    section Arrow Functions
      User: --> Simplified syntax
      User: --> Context binding;
